questions.subject: Polity
questions.prompt: The 'India Justice Report', which recently evaluated the functioning of the Indian Juvenile Justice System, is an initiative led by which organization?

options.text: NITI Aayog
Tata Trusts
National Commission for Protection of Child Rights (NCPCR)
Ministry of Social Justice and Empowerment

questions.explanation: The India Justice Report is led by Tata Trusts in collaboration with various civil society organizations to provide data-driven insights into the formal justice system.
questions.takeaway: The India Justice Report is a prominent non-governmental assessment of India's justice delivery system.
questions.evidenceExcerpt: Led by Tata Trusts, in collaboration with civil society organizations, The India Justice Report provides data-driven insights to track improvements and deficits in the formal justice system.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.prompt: A new digital platform for tribal education mentioned in the report offers 'Psychometric Assessments' for students. This specific initiative is inspired by which of the following?

options.text: The 'PISA' framework of OECD
NCERT’s ‘Tamanna’ initiative
The 'DIKSHA' portal of the Ministry of Education
The 'ASPIRE' scheme of MSME

questions.explanation: The digital platform for tribal education offers psychometric assessments inspired by NCERT’s ‘Tamanna’ initiative.
questions.takeaway: Tribal education initiatives are increasingly integrating psychometric tools modeled after established NCERT frameworks.
questions.evidenceExcerpt: It is an innovative digital platform which offers Psychometric Assessments: Inspired by NCERT’s ‘Tamanna’ initiative.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.prompt: The Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Amendment Act, 2021, introduced a significant shift in the administrative handling of adoption cases. Which of the following best describes this change compared to the 2015 Act?

options.text: It transferred the power to issue adoption orders from the Civil Courts to the District Magistrate.
It shifted the oversight of adoption from the Central Adoption Resource Authority (CARA) to the State Juvenile Justice Boards.
It mandated that adoption orders must be finalized by High Courts instead of District Courts to ensure judicial scrutiny.
It replaced the role of the Child Welfare Committee with the District Magistrate in determining the 'legal free' status of a child.

questions.explanation: Under the original JJ Act 2015, adoption orders were issued by civil courts. The 2021 Amendment empowered District Magistrates (including Additional District Magistrates) to issue these orders to ensure speedy disposal of cases and enhance accountability.
questions.takeaway: The 2021 JJ Amendment decentralized and executive-led the adoption process by moving it from the judiciary (Civil Courts) to the executive (District Magistrates).
questions.evidenceExcerpt: Comparison between JJ Act 2015 and JJ Amendment Act, 2021: Adoption - JJ Act, 2015: Adoption of a child is final once a civil court issues an adoption order. JJ Amendment Act, 2021: Instead of the court, the District Magistrate (including Additional District Magistrate) will now issue such adoption orders.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.prompt: In the institutional framework of the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015, how is the jurisdiction of Juvenile Justice Boards (JJBs) distinguished from Child Welfare Committees (CWCs)?

options.text: JJBs handle children above 18 years involved in crime, while CWCs handle children below 18 years.
JJBs deal with children in conflict with law, whereas CWCs deal with children in need of care and protection.
JJBs are responsible for the adoption process, while CWCs are responsible for the trial of heinous offences.
JJBs are state-level oversight bodies, while CWCs are district-level implementation units for tribal education.

questions.explanation: The JJ Act 2015 creates a clear distinction: Juvenile Justice Boards (JJBs) are constituted for children alleged to have committed an offence (conflict with law), while Child Welfare Committees (CWCs) are established to handle children who require care, protection, and rehabilitation.
questions.takeaway: The JJ Act bifurcates the justice system based on the child's status: 'in conflict with law' (JJB) vs 'in need of care and protection' (CWC).
questions.evidenceExcerpt: Juvenile Justice Boards (JJBs): Constituted for children in conflict with law. Child Welfare Committees (CWCs): Conduct inquiries for children in need of care and protection, declare fit persons for care, and order foster care or rehabilitation measures.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.prompt: Under the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Amendment Act, 2021, 'serious offences' (offences with imprisonment between 3 to 7 years) were reclassified. What is the primary legal implication of this change for a juvenile?

options.text: Such offences are now treated as 'heinous offences' allowing the juvenile to be tried as an adult.
The offences have been made 'cognizable', allowing police to arrest the juvenile without a warrant.
The offences have been made 'non-cognizable', meaning the police require a warrant to make an arrest.
The minimum age for criminal responsibility for such offences was raised from 7 to 12 years.

questions.explanation: The 2021 Amendment reclassified 'serious offences' (3-7 years imprisonment) as non-cognizable. Previously, they were cognizable. This change was intended to protect children from the immediate rigors of the criminal justice system unless a warrant is issued.
questions.takeaway: The 2021 JJ Amendment shifted the nature of 'serious offences' from cognizable to non-cognizable to provide better protection to juveniles.
questions.evidenceExcerpt: JJ Amendment Act, 2021: Serious offences (offences with imprisonment between 3-7 years) will be non-cognizable.
